params.text:
Making mealtimes better

How a meal is served matters as much as what is on the plate. Person-centred mealtime support starts with offering genuine choice, creating a pleasant, calm environment, and giving people the time and help they need without taking over.

  • Choice: what to eat, how much, and where to sit.
  • Environment: calm, unhurried and free of clutter and distractions.
  • Positioning: sitting upright and comfortable to eat safely.

Support and independence

Many services use protected mealtimes, where non-urgent tasks pause so staff can focus on helping people eat. When you assist someone, sit down at their eye level, go at their pace and never rush them.

Encourage people to do as much as they can for themselves, and use assistive equipment where it helps — adapted cutlery, plate guards, non-slip mats and special cups can all support independence and dignity. Good positioning, sitting upright, also helps people swallow safely.

params.question: When helping someone to eat, you should sit down at their level, go at their pace and encourage them to do as much as they can for themselves.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Sitting down, going at the person's pace and supporting their independence is good person-centred practice.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Sitting at their level, not rushing, and supporting independence keeps mealtimes safe and dignified.
